annotations.label: NP-complete problem (informal)

anchor.snippet: a problem is NP-complete when

annotations.note: Mathlib has computability theory but no complexity-class hierarchy (P, NP, NP-complete).

annotations.label: Class NP

anchor.snippet: Problems that meet the first three criteria belong to the class NP

annotations.note: No definition of the complexity class NP exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-complete (formal)

anchor.section: Formal definition and related complexity classes
anchor.snippet: is NP-complete if

annotations.note: No formal NP-completeness predicate exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class P

anchor.section: Formal definition and related complexity classes
anchor.snippet: A decision problem that is solvable in polynomial time belongs to the class P

annotations.note: No notion of polynomial-time complexity class P exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-hard

anchor.section: Formal definition and related complexity classes
anchor.snippet: A problem is said to be NP-hard if everything in NP can be transformed in polynomial time into it

annotations.note: No NP-hardness predicate exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cook–Levin theorem

anchor.snippet: The Cook–Levin theorem states that the Boolean satisfiability problem is NP-complete

annotations.note: Neither SAT nor NP-completeness exist in Mathlib, so Cook–Levin is absent.

annotations.label: Karp's 21 NP-complete problems

anchor.snippet: Richard Karp proved that several other problems were also NP-complete

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complexity-theoretic results in the Karp lineage.

annotations.label: List of NP-complete problems

anchor.snippet: The list below contains some well-known problems that are NP-complete when expressed as decision problems

annotations.note: No catalogue of NP-complete problems exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 3-SAT vs 2-SAT vs Max-2-SAT

anchor.snippet: the 3-satisfiability problem, a restriction of the Boolean satisfiability problem, remains NP-complete

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize SAT, 2-SAT, 3-SAT, or their complexities.

annotations.label: Graph coloring boundary

anchor.snippet: Determining whether a graph can be colored with 2 colors is in P, but with 3 colors is NP-complete

annotations.note: Graph colorability is formalized via `SimpleGraph.Colorable`, but no complexity classification exists.

annotations.label: Cycle/bipartite vs maximum subgraph

anchor.snippet: Determining if a graph is a cycle or is bipartite is very easy

annotations.note: Bipartiteness and cycle predicates exist but no complexity-class statements about them.

annotations.label: Knapsack approximation vs exact

anchor.snippet: A solution of the knapsack problem within any fixed percentage of the optimal solution can be computed in polynomial time

annotations.note: The knapsack problem and approximation schemes are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph isomorphism as NP-intermediate

anchor.snippet: An interesting example is the graph isomorphism problem

annotations.note: Mathlib defines graph isomorphism but says nothing about its complexity status.

annotations.label: Existence of NP-intermediate problems

anchor.snippet: This class is called NP-Intermediate problems and exists if and only if P≠NP

annotations.note: Ladner's theorem and the P vs NP question are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Superpolynomial time for known NP-complete algorithms

anchor.snippet: all known algorithms for NP-complete problems require time that is superpolynomial in the input size

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of runtime/complexity to express superpolynomial bounds.

annotations.label: Polynomial-time Turing reduction

anchor.section: Completeness under different types of reduction
anchor.snippet: is polynomial-time Turing-reducible to a problem

annotations.note: Mathlib defines plain Turing reducibility with no resource bounds, not the polynomial-time variant.

annotations.label: Logarithmic-space many-one reduction

anchor.section: Completeness under different types of reduction
anchor.snippet: the logarithmic-space many-one reduction which is a many-one reduction that can be computed with only a logarithmic amount of space

annotations.note: Mathlib has unbounded many-one reducibility but no log-space resource-bounded variant.

annotations.label: Log-space implies polynomial-time reduction

anchor.section: Completeness under different types of reduction
anchor.snippet: every computation that can be done in logarithmic space can also be done in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib has no space- or time-bounded computation model to express L ⊆ P.

annotations.label: AC0 reductions strictly weaker

anchor.section: Completeness under different types of reduction
anchor.snippet: reductions define a strictly smaller class than polynomial-time reductions

annotations.note: AC0 and circuit complexity are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Presburger arithmetic requires more than exponential time

anchor.snippet: some problems have been proven to require more time, for example Presburger arithmetic

annotations.note: Presburger arithmetic and its time-complexity lower bounds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Halting problem unsolvable

anchor.snippet: they can never be solved at all, for example the halting problem

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `halting_problem`: the predicate `(eval c n).Dom` is not a `ComputablePred`.

annotations.label: Valiant–Vazirani theorem

anchor.snippet: there are NP-problems with at most one solution that are NP-hard under randomized polynomial-time reduction

annotations.note: Randomized reductions and NP are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subexponential algorithms via planar separator

anchor.snippet: the independent set and dominating set problems for planar graphs are NP-complete, but can be solved in subexponential time using the planar separator theorem

annotations.note: Planar separator theorem and planar-graph algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial-time algorithms must err often unless P=NP

anchor.snippet: any polynomial-time algorithm must asymptotically be wrong on more than polynomially many of the exponentially many inputs of a certain size

annotations.note: No complexity-theoretic average-case results live in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BQP and NP-completeness

anchor.snippet: BQP is not believed to contain all of NP, and if it does not, then it cannot contain any NP-complete problem

annotations.note: BQP and quantum complexity classes are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-closure of NPC

anchor.snippet: the set NPC of all NP-complete problems is not closed under

annotations.note: Without NP-completeness in Mathlib, closure properties of NPC cannot be stated.

annotations.label: NPC closure under complement equivalent to NP=co-NP

anchor.snippet: It is not known whether NPC is closed under complementation

annotations.note: Neither NP nor co-NP are formalized in Mathlib.
